text:
Built an Atlassian MCP server for Claude that won't silently destroy your Confluence pages.

The pattern with every existing Atlassian MCP server I've tried - including Atlassian's own: when you ask Claude to edit a Jira issue or Confluence page that contains panels, mentions, status badges, or layouts, the round-trip loses them. No error, no warning. Just quietly mangled pages.

Atlassian's own bug tracker has had this open for months. Their fix on the Jira side was to make the LLM author raw ADF JSON trees directly - workable for a model, brutal as a UX. The Confluence side still has several open issues for the same class of bug.

omni-dev takes a different approach: an extended Markdown dialect with directives for the ADF-only constructs (panels, mentions, layouts, decisions, status badges), with every conversion checked against Atlassian's published schema. If a node would be lost in the round-trip, the conversion fails loudly with a structured error pointing at the offending JSON path - instead of silently dropping content.

~45 MCP tools across Jira and Confluence, destructive-command guards, written in Rust. Install with `cargo install omni-dev --features mcp` or via the public Docker image at https://github.com/orgs/rust-works/packages/container/package/omni-dev-mcp.

Posted a full writeup on r/mcp with setup details and a tour of the directive syntax:
https://www.reddit.com/r/mcp/comments/1tam8tz/omnidev_atlassian_jiraconfluence_mcp_server_with/

Repo: https://github.com/rust-works/omni-dev

Re-reading HPMOR...

In chapter 18, Harry wins a fight with Professor Snape. He pushes harder than Snape can push back. He uses his fame. He makes threats. He uses his time turner to escape in a way no first year is supposed to. He wins the fight clean.

Then he loses unfettered access to the time turner. Professor McGonagall restricts it because of what he did with it. The thing that made him powerful is now locked down. He won the fight and lost the war, and did not see it coming.

Then in chapter 19, Professor Quirrell pulls him aside and teaches him how to lose. Not how to avoid losing. How to lose on purpose, in small ways, so you do not burn the things you actually need.

Because someone who cannot bear to lose any fight has a weakness. Enemies don't have to beat them. They just keep picking fights the person cannot walk away from. Every "win" costs something real, and sometimes the cost is the thing that was making you win.

This is the US and Iran right now.

Both have set things up so any visible backing down looks like surrender. Iran cannot look weak in front of its allies. The US cannot look weak in front of its allies either. So every small fight has to be won.

But each win burns something. Ships. Money. Sanctions that used to bite. Allies who used to pick up the phone. Oil routes that used to be quiet. The winning quietly eats the things they need to keep winning.

Pride kills. Sometimes you have to swallow a small loss to avoid losing the thing that was making you strong in the first place.

In any case, HPMOR is recommended read.
It's possible that Trump had to win his second term in order to fuck things up so badly in order for serious electoral reform to succeed.
Humanity's biggest challenge is coming soon and we only have one shot to solve it and we are not prepared.
